participants.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official participant criteria and population closely, including the tribes involved, child age range, home setting, exclusion of major physical or behavioral disorders, and family-level randomization.
participants.extracted: Eligible participants were American Indian (Indigenous) families from four Wisconsin tribes—Menominee, Lac du Flambeau, Bad River, and Oneida—recruited primarily through Head Start programs and community outreach. Children were required to be 2 to 5 years old, living with at least one primary caregiver in a home setting, free of major physical or behavioral disorders that would seriously limit participation, and willing to accept randomization to either group. Primary caregivers also had to agree to participate in home-based lessons and group sessions, complete questionnaires and physical/laboratory screening, and have their child undergo basic screening. Families were randomized at the family level after baseline assessment, with stratification by child BMI percentile (<85th vs ≥85th percentile).

Exclusion criteria: children with presence of major physical or behavioural conditions that would preclude participation.
Indigenous population: Bad River Band of Lake Superior Chippewa Indians, the Lac du Flambeau Band of Lake Superior Chippewa Indians, the Menominee Nation, and the Oneida Nation
Setting: USA, reservation
Place of delivery: Indian Health Service and Head Start sites

interventions.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official intervention on the core design, delivery format, target population, content, duration, and mentor role, with only minor paraphrasing.
interventions.extracted: Healthy Children, Strong Families (HCSF) was a 2-year, family-based, culturally adapted home-mentoring intervention for Indigenous American Indian children aged 2–5 years and their primary caregivers. In Year 1, intervention families received 12 mentor-led home visits using a toolkit curriculum focused on four healthy lifestyle goals: increasing fruit and vegetable intake, decreasing soda/sweetened drinks and candy, increasing physical activity, and decreasing TV viewing time. Lessons included goal setting, self-monitoring, skill building, culturally appropriate incentives, and support for both parent and child behavior change. Intervention families also attended three mentor-led group sessions with extended family involvement, covering nutrition education, hands-on cooking, and physical activities. In Year 2, intervention families continued with monthly group meetings and monthly newsletters with parenting tips, recipes, and local program information to support maintenance of behavior changes. Mentors were tribal members or community-connected individuals trained extensively to deliver home-visiting support and coaching, with an emphasis on self-efficacy, parenting skills, and traditional cultural values around food and activity.
interventions.official:
Intervention
Intervention name: Mentored Health Child, Strong Families (Mentored HCSF)
Intervention aim: HCSF aims to change behaviours through increased knowledge of healthy lifestyles, enhanced parenting and increased self‐efficacy.
Theory used to develop intervention:
HCSF is based on social cognitive and family systems theories and seeks to change behaviours at the family level.
Consumer and community involvement: tool kit lessons and activities were developed by the University of Wisconsin–

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes include many additional primary and secondary measures and multiple time points beyond the official record, which lists only BMI z-score and SF-12 outcomes at 12 months.
outcomes.extracted: Primary outcomes: child BMI z-score change from baseline to 1-year post-intervention and 2-year post-intervention, measured from height and weight with conversion to CDC BMI percentiles/z-scores; adult primary caregiver BMI change from baseline to 1-year post-intervention and 2-year post-intervention, measured from height and weight. Secondary outcomes planned for both children and primary caregivers included nutrition behaviors measured at baseline, 1 year post, and 2 years post using three non-consecutive 24-hour dietary recalls (including one weekend day) and the Block Food Questionnaire (Block Kids 2–7; baseline subset and post-intervention), specifically fruit/vegetable intake and soda/sweetened drink and candy intake; screen time measured at baseline, 1 year post, and 2 years post using recall-based questions about TV on in the home, time watching TV/videos/DVDs, and for children time spent playing video games or using a computer; and physical activity measured at baseline, 1 year post, and 2 years post using Actical accelerometers worn for 7 days (minimum 5 days with at least 10 hours/day). Additional planned outcomes for primary caregivers included anthropometric measures at baseline, 1 year post, and 2 years post: waist circumference, height, weight, and BMI; biochemical measures in a subset at baseline, 1 year post, and 2 years post including 75-g oral glucose tolerance test/fasting blood glucose, fasting lipid profile, C-reactive protein, urinary microalbumin, and creatinine; Health Behavior Efficacy questionnaire scores at baseline, 1 year post, and 2 years post; SF-12v2 Health Survey physical and mental component and subscale scores at baseline, 1 year post, and 2 years post; and Cultural Involvement Scale at baseline only.
outcomes.official:
Overall health and well‐being: BMI z‐score
Child physical health and development: BMI z‐score
Family enhancing lifestyle or behaviour outcomes: 12‐item Short Form Survey (SF‐12) Physical Health
Parent/carer psychological health: SF‐12 Mental Health
Time points: 12 months
Funding source and conflicts of interest
